@lukesneeringer I'm back at this, but can't figure out what the desired resolution would be.  Here is a sketch at a way forward:

- Return `frozenset` instances from the getters for `owners`, `editors`, and `viewers` (breaking anybody who expects to mutate them in place, rather than re-assigning them).
- Emit `DeprecationWarning` from the setters for those properties, pointing people to the desired surface.

Then, the question is what the desired surface would be:  I'm really leery of exposing the mutable `bindings` attribute as a dict:  it allows us no way to do any checking at all.  We could:

- Make the `Policy` class dict-like, with roles as keys and lists of principals as values.  The `__setitem__` implementation would give us the hook to do any required checking.

- Make `Policy.bindings` a dict-like object with a similar `__setitem__`.

- Add a `Policy.bind(role, *principals)` method, and use whatever backing store we like.

FYI, @itdxer: the `PyCapsule` API replaced the legacy `PyCObject` API (a while ago)… so this is a fundamental low-level object.  The change to `PyCapsule` happened a while back… so I'm a bit surprised to be only seeing this issue in `python3.4` and not earlier or in earlier versions… however, `python3.4` uses very different pickling.

I'll have to do some digging, and also work to reproduce the error you are seeing in this issue.

Here's the deal… they sort-of are picklable, and sort-of aren't.
The reason `dill.pickles` says `False` is that modifications to the class `dict` make it "different" than what you have coded.  You can see this a little more clearly if you look at this:

    >>> dill.detect.errors(b1)
    AssertionError("Unpickling produces <class '__main__.Bunch'> instead of <class '__main__.Bunch'>",)

Ah… well that makes sense.  `dill` is pretty good at handling all the different decorator patterns on functions, but there are a lot more pitfalls on decorated classes.  I just looked, and I don't think I even test against decorated classes… but I do know there are serialization failures of all sorts when classes monkey with `__init__` and/or `__new__`.

If you could come up with a minimal case of your decorator that fails (i.e. one not requiring your package) and fill out a `dill` ticket, then that's probably the best chance that there'll be a general solution implemented to fix your particular issue.  That's suggestion (1) in my previous message. 

I think the faster workaround might be to add a `__reduce__` method to the class.  That's suggestion (2).  Yes, it's a one-off to solve the problem for that specific class… but then you'd not have to wait for `dill` to implement the general solution.  Although, a `dill` ticket with the reduced case of your issue would be much appreciated.

Hi @cmberryau, 

The ADAL python library expects the authority URL of the format `https://login.windows.net/common`. It essentially uses this authority URL value to form the URL to the token endpoint. So, when the authority_url value is `https://login.windows.net/common/oauth2/authorize` as you provided, it ends up forming an invalid URL to the token endpoint making the POST(token request) a 404. 

**Why this works with ADAL .Net?**
The .NET library is more forgiving to the authority URL that is provided because it internally constructs the correct one.

So, this is definitely an enhancement that we can include in the future releases of the python library :)

Ran experiments to evaluate different serialization format.

* Serialization format evaluated: `pickle`, `msgpack`, `json`.

* Experiment setup

  Run `./pants list :: --enable-v2-engine` on twitter's monorepo and save serialized pickle objects to local `Lmdb ` storage (~`360MB` in size, 880k key/value pairs), then in a separate process load those objects into memory, run them through different serializers.

* Evaluation result

  Footprint. At 400bytes per key/value pair, compression isn't useful, we are able to achieve 50% compression ratio overall at the cost of twice of much time.
  
  Performance. Pickle surprisingly performs the best. Roughly pickle `20%` faster than `msgpack`, which is `20%` faster than `json`. Notice our data is about 50 times smaller than the `Uber` test setup. I increased our value size by making them `x` times bigger and the break even point appears to be around `100k`, beyond that `msgpack` performs the best. There might be other factors and size being one of them.

* Action items

1. Clean up `lmdb` storage, no long necessary.
2. Replace `key` to `serialized` object mapping with `key` to `original` object mapping.
3. Continue to use `pickle` for serialization for now, will wait for improvement from other areas first. (that reduces #object)

This is a complex issue; I totally agree with your reasoning. 

Error handling in SATOSA needs a complete overhaul, IMO. 

For start, exceptions that are caught and then raise other exceptions should follow the chain pattern (`raise ... from ...`) that python provides.
Then, I would try to group errors in domains or subsystems and form a structure.
Then, it should be easier to add or remove the needed exception/error classes.
This would allow for better error messages and logging (which is by itself another big refactor). 

```python
# generic application error
# all custom error/exceptions should derive from this
class AppError(Exception):
  pass

class SomeFuncError(AppError):
  pass

# an error that describes errors in a subsystem
# an example would be AuthenticationError
# error messages would specify if it is due to invalid token or wrong password etc
# another example would be AuthorisationError/PermissionError
# error messages would specify if it is due to invalid token or wrong password etc
class SubSystemError(AppError):
  pass

# subsystems may provide lower level exceptions
# an example would be a BasicAuthenticationError
# either because no such user was found or because the password did not match
class SomeOtherFuncError(SubSystemError):
  pass

def some_other_func():
  raise SomeOtherFuncError("error in low level operation occured")

def some_func():
  try:
    some_other_func()
  except SubSystemError as e:
    raise SomeFuncError("failed to do some_func") from e

def main():
  try:
    some_func()
  except AppError as e:
    log.error("Application error", exc_info=settings.DEBUG)
  except Exception as e:
    log.exception("System error")
```

This allows application-errors to be logged by their error message only, unless the debug flag is set. 
When debug is set, one gets a full stacktrace to the point that caused the first exception that was not handled. 

In general I would argue that always showing stacktraces *is* useful, but I am not opposed to hiding them for cosmetic reasons - however a mechanism to enable them should be in place, in order to ask for them when a user can reproduce a problem; and that often happens in QA forums or GH-issues.

import soco


class HttpServer(Thread):
    """A simple HTTP Server in its own thread"""

    def __init__(self, port):
        super(HttpServer, self).__init__()
        self.daemon = True
        handler = SimpleHTTPRequestHandler
        self.httpd = TCPServer(("", port), handler)

    def run(self):
        """Start the server"""
        print('Start HTTP server')
        self.httpd.serve_forever()

    def stop(self):
        """Stop the server"""
        print('Stop HTTP server')
        self.httpd.socket.close()

@thomas-mangin, many thanks for your detailed answer.

My understanding is that a "SHALL" statement represents a mandatory requirement for an implementation...

> MUST   This word, or the terms "REQUIRED" or "SHALL", mean that the definition is an absolute requirement of the specification.

([RFC2119](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2119))

... so I wanted to open this issue, first of all to be sure my analysis of ExaBGP's behaviour was right and then, in case you were not already aware of it, to let you know about that.

You say that SHALL is not a mandatory requirement for compliance; I'm not deeply involved in IETF practices, so of course I'm missing something here: could you point me to the right interpretation of the word within this context? Anyhow I apologize If I wasted your time with this.

WRT the choice of error handling approach, I actually agree (for what it's worth) with the treat-as-withdraw-only approach: given the options from [RFC7606 section 2](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7606#section-2) IMHO it's the one that best fits. The first two options would lead to the same (or similar) behaviour you described in the background you provided about ASN4, while the fourth one ("attribute discard") could lead to opposite or counterproductive results respect those a BGP speaker would have desired. The same "attribute discard" option's description states that `This approach MUST NOT be used except in the case of an attribute that has no effect on route selection or installation`, while the whole large BGP communities is exactly meant `to aid in routing management` and, from our daily experience, we know communities are <del>widely</del> _largely_ (:smile:) used to influence route selection or installation. [RFC7606 section 7.8](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7606#section-7.8) and [section 8](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7606#section-8) (`In particular, if the attribute in question has or may have an effect on route selection or installation, the presumption is that discarding it is unsafe unless careful analysis proves otherwise.`) also face this specific issue.

My (unrequested) two cents. If you use the entire dataset to build the vocabulary, of course your test set is going to have a smaller percentage of OOV tokens than normal and that is a bias. It is probably very small, given that OOV tokens are going to be very rare tokens for any reasonably-sized vocabulary, but it's there.

Sure, you can always augment your ``Embedding`` layer to include other pre-trained word vectors from the same set you used during training. Okay, but you should probably be aware of the fact that your test performance is not biased only if you actually do that before you use your model with new data (if possible) or you use the entire set of pre-trained embeddings after training. It's a biased performance wrt the particular instance of ``Model`` you trained. 

Does it make sense?

(*) In practice, I guess the bias quickly decreases with the amount of pre-trained vectors (sorted by frequency) included in the embedding matrix.

Without closer inspection, my best guess would be that the ODBC driver returns a very large field size for `NTEXT` columns.

If the returned size is something like 1 billion, this means that 1,000,000,000 * 2 (for unicode) bytes are required to buffer a single value. Because the default read buffer size is set to 20 MB and this field alone exceeds it, it will create a buffer with just one row. For asynchronous IO, a second buffer is needed. Usually, the buffer is halved in order to keep the total read buffer size constant. But a buffer of just 1 row cannot be halved, thus leading to doubling the required memory to 4 GB.

This would be consistent with the information given here: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/t-sql/data-types/ntext-text-and-image-transact-sql.

After confirmation, I would have to see whether there is a more clever way to transfer text data than doing the same thing as for `VARCHAR`.

This leaves me with the original idea, which is to add a mechanism to bypass the journal.  After taking a look at the code, I think this is quite doable and you should be able to validate this approach pretty easily with a basic test.

The `JournalDB` will need to be updated to allow bypassing the journal.  I think the best way to do this would be to add a `bypass_journal` flag to all of the `get/set/delete` methods which defaults to `False`

When `bypass_journal` is `True` the journal should do the following.

- proceed with the operation on the `self.wrapped_db`
- *if* the journal has any checkpoints, the earliest checkpoint should be updated to contain the new value for the key in question.

This code can be tested with the following somewhat simple test cases.

```python
jdb = JournalDB(...)

# case 1
s = jdb.snapshot()
jdb.set(k, v, bypass=True)
jdb.revert(s)
assert jdb.get(k) == v

# case 2
s = jdb.snapshot()
jdb.set(k, v)
jdb.set(k, v2, bypass=True)
jdb.revert(s)
assert jdb.get(k) == v2

# case 3
s = jdb.snapshot()
jdb.set(k, v, bypass=True)
jdb.set(k, v2)
jdb.revert(s)
assert jdb.get(k) == v

# case 4
s = jdb.snapshot()
jdb.set(k, v, bypass=True)
s2 = jdb.snapshot()
jdb.set(k, v2)
jdb.revert(s)
assert jdb.get(k) == v
```

Let me know what you think about this approach.  It feels cleaner than intertwining the actual PAYGAS business logic into different layers of the stack.  There will also need to be a change to the `AccountStateDB` object itself to tell it to bypass the journal which should probably just be a flag passed to it during instantiation.

@pipermerriam @boneyard93501 I compared the execution time between requesting the transaction receipts for a particular block synchronously and asynchronously. I used `asyncio` and `aiohttp` module for async HTTP requests. I based my code on [the excellent article](https://pawelmhm.github.io/asyncio/python/aiohttp/2016/04/22/asyncio-aiohttp.html) by @pawelmhm. 
The results are very promising. When the program is run locally on a machine with a running parity node I get:
```
sync: 1.843s
async: 0.264s
```
When it's run over the Internet the results are:
```
sync: 8.701s
async: 0.757s
```
Overall I get 7x-11x execution time improvement if the requests are batched and sent asynchronously. My code below:
```python
import timeit
import asyncio

from aiohttp import ClientSession

from web3.providers.base import JSONBaseProvider
from web3.providers import HTTPProvider
from web3 import Web3

# synchronously request receipts for given transactions
def sync_receipts(web3, transactions):
    for tran in transactions:
        web3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tran)

# asynchronous JSON RPC API request
async def async_make_request(session, url, method, params):
    base_provider = JSONBaseProvider()
    request_data = base_provider.encode_rpc_request(method, params)
    async with session.post(url, data=request_data,
                        headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'}) as response:
        content = await response.read()
    response = base_provider.decode_rpc_response(content)
    return response

async def run(node_address, transactions):
    tasks = []

    # Fetch all responses within one Client session,
    # keep connection alive for all requests.
    async with ClientSession() as session:
        for tran in transactions:
            task = asyncio.ensure_future(async_make_request(session, node_address,
                                                            'eth_getTransactionReceipt',[tran.hex()]))
            tasks.append(task)

        responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    eth_node_address = "http://localhost:8545"
    web3 = Web3(HTTPProvider(eth_node_address))

    block = web3.eth.getBlock(web3.eth.blockNumber)
    transactions = block['transactions']

    start_time = timeit.default_timer()
    sync_receipts(web3, transactions)
    print('sync: {:.3f}s'.format(timeit.default_timer() - start_time))

    start_time = timeit.default_timer()
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    future = asyncio.ensure_future(run(eth_node_address, transactions))
    loop.run_until_complete(future)
    print('async: {:.3f}s'.format(timeit.default_timer() - start_time))
```

The problem occurs because no other than root user has access to read the these two files:
```bash
[root@40b7f7d32436 ~] ls -l /opt/anaconda/pkgs/dbus-1.10.10-0/bin/.dbus-post-link.sh
-rwxr-x--- 2 root root 766 Sep 13  2016 /opt/anaconda/pkgs/dbus-1.10.10-0/bin/.dbus-post-link.sh

[root@40b7f7d32436 ~] ls -l /opt/anaconda/pkgs/dbus-1.10.10-0/libexec/dbus-daemon-launch-helper
-rwxr-x--- 1 root root 176268 Sep 13  2016 /opt/anaconda/pkgs/dbus-1.10.10-0/libexec/dbus-daemon-launch-helper
```
Workaround is simple. As root user, grant read privileges. 
```bash
[root@40b7f7d32436 ~] chmod +r /opt/anaconda/pkgs/dbus-1.10.10-0/bin/.dbus-post-link.sh
[root@40b7f7d32436 ~] chmod +r /opt/anaconda/pkgs/dbus-1.10.10-0/libexec/dbus-daemon-launch-helper
```

This changes makes it difficult to know what packages are installed with pip and which ones with conda!  The output of both "conda list" and "conda list --no-pip" are exactly the same and there is no way to know which packages are installed with pip so they can be upgraded with pip.

I would have been fine if conda managed all package management under the hood and I did not have to worry about what packages are installed with pip and which ones with conda so I can upgrade and maintain them easily.
Actually swapping the two out will not be hard, but we should take a look at how the two differ and how that will affect the existing packages.

@ukoethe some background info:

> I don't quite understand why all msvc_runtime variants have version numbers 1.0.0 or 1.0.1

Because it's a single package that bundles different DLLs based on the VC version used to build the recipe.  Splitting it into separate recipes is certainly an option, especially if it smooths out these dependency resolution issues.  However, it introduces pinning issues that might be annoying.  Any library needs both features, and now more conditional code for which msvc_runtime package it should depend on.  Your jinja2 stuff would make this much nicer, but we have a lot of work to do internally to modernize recipes to use jinja2.  That's probably the long-term fix.

In the current system, track-features lives in Python - nowhere else.  Python 2.7 tracks vc9; Python 3.4 tracks vc10, Python 3.5 tracks vc14.  This metadata ends up in repodata.json ( http://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/win-32/repodata.json or http://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/win-64/repodata.json)

Both of these look consistent - all the vc versions match Python versions.

msvc_runtime has no dependencies whatsoever.  I am lost on this.  Pinging @mcg1969, our solver expert.

When you print the whole variant, pysam is calling htslib's `vcf_format` which formats the QUAL column with C's `sprintf("%g")`.

When you use `print variant.qual`, you're getting Python's default floating point formatting, which is probably fairly raw.  To compare like with like and display this same value with similar formatting and precision, you will want to format it yourself with whatever Python's equivalent of `%g` is.

Thanks again for your response (and for having the patience with me). I still don't understand it well so let me try to illustrate this another way.

Suppose I have 2 async workers (worker A and B). They both try to update the same row (a user full name).

```
s = Session()
try:
   u = s.query(User).get(user_id)
   u.name = 'Joe ' + worker_name  
   u.add(x)
   u.commit()
except NotAConflictException:
   u.rollback()
finally:
  u.close()
```

Now A and B get there, select the user row and now A is first to update the name and to a `SAVEPOINT RELEASE` and B is behind and gets the conflict error. 

In PG `worker B` will just wait until the lock is released from that row and update with the new value (overwriting the prev value) so in this scenario the name will be `Joe B`. I guess it is open for discussion if this is 'correct' or not. In some cases this is fine, in others if I wanted to make sure there is no accidental overwriting I would use `WITH FOR UPDATE` or some other synchronization method.

If I understood anything from our discussion in CRDB the above would raise a conflict and allow the app to retry the same transaction (optionally) checking that I'm not overwriting anything. I'm also guessing that this exacerbated by CRDB because it needs to sync between nodes so it could be that it's not really a different client doing work on that row but a CRDB node - so the conflict is more likely. 

While I think that is the 'correct' way to do it (probably for any db) - it's verbose and I wonder why not use the [Automatic Retries](https://www.cockroachlabs.com/docs/stable/transactions.html#automatic-retries) by default and also allow the developer to decide where to use the retry logic. Does that make sense?

Turns out you need a `--source-sas` param. I managed to get things working with the following:

```bash
az storage blob copy start-batch \
    --source-account "$source_account" \
    --destination-container system \
    --source-container system \
    --prefix "$source_blob" \
    --account-name "$destination_account" \
    --source-sas "$source_sas"
```

Where the `source_sas` is generated with the following:


>I will sometimes see accounts from both login credentials.
This is correct behavior. If you use cli to login to 2 accounts, it will remember/cache all subscriptions from 2 accounts

   > created my new kubernetes cluster the first time I ran the command `az acs ...`

I assume to use ` --service-principal` argument to create service principal on the fly? Or you have logged in as a service principal and create acs? 

  >it failed because it couldn't find the resource group but not until after it had done some SP provisioning.

You might have to ensure you are under right active subscription by using `az account set`

  > My guess is that it somehow created another new SP in a different account/subscription than the one in which it created the actual cluster and associated the kubernetes 
SP is a tenant specific entity which is not tied to a subscription. A single tenant can managed multiple subscriptions. For you situation, your multiple subscriptions will belong to one of the 2 tenants, one from your corporate account, and the other from you MSDN one.  When you you use `az account set` to ensure you are in right subscription and tenant, the SP will be created under that (tenant, subscription) pair. As long as you create `acs` under subscriptions belonging to the same tenant, it should work.

I agree this is not very clear. Couple of explanations here:
The `display-name` is not useful for service principal created for RBAC, and in cli you probably will never use it. Because of this, we associate `-n and --name` with `app-id-uri` which people will use the same value to login or assign roles to it. This is not a perfect association, but in AAD graph database, the `app-id-uri` is mapped to one of the `servicePrincipalNames`, so the name still makes some sense. Also we don't want to expose too many arguments from this command which has already got too many.

This is an excellent question, and this has moved a lot recently. Today, my response would be:
- Use `az login` to authenticate using Service Principal to the CLI (do not use interactive login)
- Use the CLI profile loader to get your client:
https://docs.microsoft.com/python/azure/python-sdk-azure-authenticate#cli-based-authentication

In a few days, you will be able to save a authentication file and load it in the SDK like:
```python
        from azure.common.client_factory import get_client_from_auth_file
        from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient
        client = get_client_from_auth_file(ComputeManagementClient)
```
I will release that probably tomorrow, it's nothing but updating release not and pushing to PyPI.

```python
client = get_client_from_auth_file(StorageManagementClient)

storage_keys = {v.key_name: v.value for v in storage_keys.keys}

```

What I'd like is for ESRI sources to be converted to some natural CSV format that's as close to the source schema as possible. Then the [conform processing code](https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/d2ca719b82d7dcf91c77d4cb1a5a8581d2d9ba62/openaddr/conform.py#L631-L636) can feed that to our `[csv_source_to_csv()](https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/d2ca719b82d7dcf91c77d4cb1a5a8581d2d9ba62/openaddr/conform.py#L403)` for CSV sources which will convert that source-native CSV to an extracted CSV, and thence to `out.csv`.

(Although really that's a bit silly; if you can write a CSV with X and Y columns, in WGS84 coordinates, and in UTF-8 then the csv-to-csv transform is a no-op. If some ESRI sources aren't in WGS84, then it'd be better to write native coordinates in the `cache.csv` file and let `csv_source_to_csv()` do the conversion.)

For Polygons and Multipolygons, just outputting the centroid as X/Y is all we need. You can probably use OGR to do the centroid calculation by creating an OGR geometry, one per row from the ESRI source. Or just do the math in Python, that's what the Node code does.

Hm... I just realized that middleware might not be a right place to override methods of requests.
I need to do resolve again, but to execute my middleware in the first place, the original request also need to be resolved. This means, I need to configure duplicate routes for both POST and other exotic methods. This may be acceptable, and doing so makes the test case happy.

Still, I fill this like a dirty hacking, because I need to use a private API (`request._match_info`) and cannot deduplicate the routes.

```python
async def api_middleware_factory(app, handler):
    async def api_middleware_handler(request):
        method_override = request.headers.get('X-Method-Override', None)
        if method_override:
            request = request.clone(method=method_override)
            # resolve again with the new exotic method
            new_match_info = await app.router.resolve(request)
            # however, the handler bound to POST is already set by aiohttp
            request._match_info = new_match_info  # need to use private API :(
        assert request.match_info is not None
        if request.match_info.http_exception is not None:
            return request.match_info.http_exception
        return (await handler(request))
    return api_middleware_handler
```
```python
async def test_api_method_override(test_client):
    observed_method = None
    app = web.Application()

    async def service_handler(request):
        nonlocal observed_method
        observed_method = request.method
        return web.Response(body=b'test')

    async def noop_handler(request):
        return web.Response(body=b'noop')

    app.router.add_route('POST', '/v{version:\d+}/version', service_handler)  # actual handler
    app.router.add_route('REPORT', '/v{version:\d+}/version', noop_handler)  # placeholder
    app.middlewares.append(api_middleware_factory)
    client = await test_client(app)

    resp = await client.post('/v2/version', headers={
        'X-Method-Override': 'REPORT',
    })
    assert resp.status == 200
    assert (await resp.read()) == b'test'
    assert observed_method == 'REPORT'

    observed_method = None
    resp = await client.delete('/v2/version')
    assert resp.status == 405
    assert observed_method is None
```

@wxsBSD I tested a bunch of different ways of fiddling with both variables at run-time, and it appears that yara throws reasonable exceptions in all of the cases If the configuration is incorrect when you run yara.compile(), then it throws an exception or crashes.

Case 1:
If you set_config properly, then run yara.compile, then change set_config to lower values, then try rule.match(), it will throw an exception (internal error 25). All of these appear to be recoverable exceptions, and even you can catch them, then run yara.set_config() to adjust, then try again - so I would say that IMO it's probably better to not make it conditional on the yr_initialize().

Case 2:
If you try compiling a large rule, it fails, then you run set_config with larger values, and then try recompiling the rule, it succeeds and matching works.

Case 3:
If you increase "max strings" but not stack_size, it seems to compile, but when you try to match, you get a code 25. If you bump the stack_size up enough, then you can match (without recompiling) and it seems to match fine, yielding results.

If you can manage a catastrophic failure case, I'd love to investigate it though. So far, it seems the ideal behavior is to allow the user to choose when they'd like to run yara.set_config.

I just wanted to jot down to suggestions.
First, I prefer the name `metadata` over `annotations` as I think it's more familiar and therefore self-explanatory.

I hesitate to add the second idea because it may be overkill, but here it is:

```python
class Metadata(Model):
    title = StringType()
    description = StringType(required=True)

class Person(Model):
    class Options:
        metadata_model = Metadata

    first_name = StringType(
        required=True,
        metadata=dict(
            title='First Name',
            description="The person's first name")
        )
    age = IntType(        
        metadata=dict(
            description="The person's age",
            as_sql_col='NUMBER')
        )
```

Basically, it's an optional mechanism to validate metadata.  I'm mostly interested in asserting the existence of required metadata, but the complete model could be useful in cases where the schema itself needs to be transmitted.

A simpler alternative (and therefore probably better):

```python
class Person(Model):
    class Options:
        required_metadata = ['description']
[...]
```

This is just an idea to ponder, take it or leave it.  

Anyway, I would love to see this issue re-opened, renamed to "Support arbitrary field metadata", and completed.  This should be really really easy, right?  If no one wants first dibs, I'll go ahead and make a PR (for the core feature, I'll leave out the metadata validation for now).

### Current file download process
Steps in downloading a file:
- ask for a download url info via GET [/api/v1/files/{id}/url](https://api.dataservice.duke.edu/apiexplorer#!/files/getApiV1FilesIdUrl)
- fetch parts of url in separate processes by adding `Range` headers to url info

We fetch the url info in the main process:
https://github.com/Duke-GCB/DukeDSClient/blob/d4d1e1c54e10ce8f0fa5d8fce2a05f1c6c1ea57d/ddsc/core/download.py#L88

I'm hesitant to add this sort of feature. The logic in [`edit_yaml.py`](https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/blob/master/peru/edit_yaml.py) is already approaching black magic, even for the relatively simple demands of reup (adding/editing scalar fields in a well-defined spot). This very minute while writing this, I thought of a case I hadn't tested before, and sure enough it turned out to be [horribly dangerously broken](https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/issues/103). The core problem is that PyYAML is not a "round trip parser": if you read in a YAML file and spit it back out, you lose any comments or formatting that the user put in there. So we have to figure out exactly which characters correspond to the value you're changing, and only replace those. YAML has a lot of corner cases, so this turns out to be error-prone.

We could probably fix all of this with enough code, but I'm also not sure it's a good problem for peru to solve. `peru reup` was a high priority for us, because editing a dozen hashes by hand is so painful, and we can do it all at once. But for the human, I'm not sure typing out a command like

```
peru module add wp-foo-plugin --type=svn --import=wp-content/plugins/foo --url=...
```

is easier than just editing the markup.

It sounds like you're talking about *scripting* a peru workflow, though. In that case, have you considered generating the `peru.yaml` file from other data, rather than trying to edit it in place? Could you tell me more about how you imagine your code being organized? Fun fact that might help: YAML is a superset of JSON, so if you can generate a JSON blob you don't even need a YAML writer.

`sAMAccountName` or `userPrincipalName` (depends on the LDAP setup) usually contain the expected username.

I have a hunch that it is related to the SysCapture method. When a `logging` StreamHandler is created, it stores a reference to the output stream (`sys.stderr`) by default, so SysCapture's redirection method has no effect. Yeah, that could work. The basket would need to be checked in a number of places to prevent basket errors:

1. When adding a new product to the basket from the catalogue pages. This is already covered because form validation uses `Basket.product_quantity()` which sums up all line quantities with the same product.
2. When editing the basket line items. The user can be making changes to multiple lines with the same product, so we have to get the basket quantities, the form quantities, and the availability. For example, a product may have 10 items available and the user currently has 5 with "Message one" and 5 with "Message two". The basket could be edited to contain 4 with "Message one" and 6 with "Message two" and should validate properly.
3. At all checkout stages to make sure the product is still available in case any stock was consumed since the product was added to the basket.

`Basket.is_quantity_allowed()` isn't currently called anywhere except during the re-order view and during the individual basket form `clean_quantity()`. I think adding a `line` argument to `Basket.is_quantity_allowed()` and then making sure it is called during form/formset cleaning would work for items 1 & 2.

For item 3 it gets a bit tricky as you said. Would the validation have to rely on the availability policy, i.e., `StockRequired.is_purchase_permitted()` or is there a good place to call `Basket.is_quantity_allowed()`? Maybe in`CheckoutSessionMixin.check_basket_is_valid()`?

The implementation of `offsets_for_times` isn't available until `v0.11.4`.
The package in pip is only `v0.11.0`.

So you can either wait for an update from pip, or, if you are impatient like me, you can compile it yourself.

Here's the example commands (tested in docker image `python:3.6`):

```
wget -q https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/archive/v0.11.4rc1.tar.gz -O /tmp/confluent-kafka.tar.gz
tar xzvf /tmp/confluent-kafka.tar.gz -C /tmp
cd /tmp/confluent-kafka-python-0.11.4rc1/
python3 setup.py install
```

Then, verify by:
```
python -c 'from confluent_kafka import version ; print(version())'
('0.11.4', 721920)
```

That said, I don't think you can call it an ISO date if you don't actually use the ISO format (which specifies `:` as a separator and uses a 24-hour clock).

I'd say it's a bug, though, since that looks like a valid datetime to me. I'm not sure what you're trying to do, but if this is the only format that's failing, a pretty simple workaround would be one like this:

```python
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil.parser import parse

dtstr = "2016-04-14T2.06.51 PM+07:00"
try:
    # You need to replace the : because %z takes a format +HHMM
    dt = datetime.strptime(dtstr.replace(':', ''), "%Y-%m-%dT%I.%H.%M %p%z")
except ValueError:
    dt = parse(dtstr)
```

This will work in Python 3. For Python 2.x, I think the best thing to do would be to manually clip off the time zone at the end, use `strptime` to get the date, and if that succeeded, `strftime` it to an actual ISO-8601 formatted date, reattach the tz portion, then pass it through the parser.

This workaroud works but it breaks [schema resolving in marshmallow extension](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/marshmallow/__init__.py#L134). The mechanism relies on Schema classes being stored, not instances.

I would like to suggest the following change to `schema_definition_helper` in marshmallow extension, so that 

- the schema instance is used to create the json description
- the schema class is stored for further reference resolving

```python
def schema_definition_helper(spec, name, schema, **kwargs):
    """Definition helper that allows using a marshmallow
    :class:`Schema <marshmallow.Schema>` to provide OpenAPI
    metadata.

    :param type schema: A marshmallow Schema class or instance.
    """

    if isinstance(schema, type):
        schema_cls = schema
        schema_instance = schema()
    else:
        schema_cls = type(schema)
        schema_instance = schema

    # Store registered refs, keyed by Schema class
    plug = spec.plugins[NAME]
    if 'refs' not in plug:
        plug['refs'] = {}
    plug['refs'][schema_cls] = name
    return swagger.schema2jsonschema(schema_instance, spec=spec, name=name)
```

Consider the case when you have deeply nested JSON structure:
`{'foo': {'bar': {'baz': {'bam': {'quux': true}}}}}`
User sends request
`{'foo': {'bap': {'baz': {'bam': {'quux': true}}}}}`
Image his confusion when he gets error like that ?!
`{'foo': {'bar': {'baz': {'bam': {'quux': ['Missing required field']}}}}}`
Then user starts complaining about API being insane and all ways broken, because the value for 'quux' is just there.

What he really should get is
`{'foo': {'bar': ['Missing required field']}}`

As with required fields, you could have a bunch of validators that will trigger only if some fields are present (e.g. validating string with regex). @falexwolf 

Since `create()` now returns nothing, you should not expect to be able to wrap it in `with` (nor is there any reason to do so, since it leaves the file closed). 

I was wrong in saying that `create()` returns `None` above, and would work in a `with` statement. It does not return any value, but if you try to use its return value anyway, the effect will be that it appears to return `None`. However, `None` is not a proper [context manager](https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Python_Programming/Context_Managers), and therefore `with` throws an `AttributeError`.

In your case, you should simply do this:

```python
import loompy
loompy.create(str(filename), adata.X.T, row_attrs=row_attrs, col_attrs=col_attrs)
```
This will create the file and leave it properly closed. If you then want to work with it, you need to do this:

```python
with loompy.connect(filename) as ds:
    # do something with the file
    print(ds.shape)
```

After the `with` block has executed, the file will again be properly closed.

> "From the standard, it's clear..."

No it's not, that's exactly the point, regardless of which of the 3 involved standards you refer to. 

Part of the SMT FP standard is to avoid as many unspecified results as possible - those create big problems for solver developers and only tiny benefits for the users. Unspecifiedness can always be recovered on the application level by guarding unspecified functions, e.g., fp.min, with an if-then-else that catches those special cases, so when we push that onto the end-user, they are responsible for picking a solver that supports uninterpreted functions; if for some reason they can't choose such a solver, then they can't have general unspecifiedness. 

I had brought the min/max +-0.0 case up with them before, but so far they haven't delivered a clear solution yet, and they may well have forgotten about it. Before the current SMT FP standard was released we used a google group to discuss all these issues, it's been quiet recently but I think it's the best place to bring this one up again, The group is called [smt-fp](https://groups.google.com/d/forum/smt-fp) and it might require getting permissions from the owners (should be quick though). 

I've taken a quick glance at BTRW14 again, and it seems like they did update the min/max paragraph to what appears like unspecifiedness, I'll confirm with them that this is indeed their intent.

Let me try to understand in concrete terms. 
You are either asking about Z3 interoperating with the allocated pages of an OS process or 
you are asking about Z3 supporting OS independent cloning mechanisms.
The second post indicates an understanding around first reading.

Based on the second reading it sounds like you would like the following basic API:
Z3_solver Z3_API Z3_solver_translate(Z3_context source, Z3_solver s, Z3_context target);

This would let you spawn a new context, and translate a solver object into the new context.
The old and new contexts can be used in different threads.
Z3's API already has Z3_ast_vector_translate, which lets you convert an ast-vector from one context to another. Z3_solver_get_assertions returns the current assertions in a solver as an ast_vector, 
so you can use these functions to directly implement Z3_solver_translate.

You can almost always serialize solvers to disk by pretty printing them to SMT-LIB2.
Are you perhaps finding this inefficient?

My overall question is whether you need more than this a solver clone mechanism?

This is because msgpack is falling back on a pure python implementation
instead of compiling a more optimized version. And for some reason the fall
back cannot handle a memory view as argument.

zerorpc should ultimately be able to work with the fallback. By detecting
it and converting the memory view to a buffer (slow but hey it's a
fallback). But I have never managed to reproduce the error on my system. (I
am on Linux).

You can try 'pip install --reinstall msgpack' and see if you can get it to
build.

Hi, thanks for your interest!

You probably know that most emulation and analysis with angr happens using a simulation manager. Lots of common scenarios involve setting up some search strategy and telling the simulation to run until some termination condition, such as finding a state at a given address, or finding a crash. One termination condition that people want to be able to use but isn't readily available is the ability to add a timeout, or that exploration should stop after a given number of seconds.

Simulation managers can have their behavior extended by writing an [Exploration Technique](http://angr.io/api-doc/angr.html#angr.exploration_techniques.ExplorationTechnique), which is a subclass of the ExplorationTechnique base class with some methods overridden. Some examples of this can be found [here](https://github.com/angr/angr/tree/master/angr/exploration_techniques). The goal of this issue is to have there be an exploration technique which causes emulation to halt after a timeout.

Probably the difficult part of this will be determining when to _start_ the timer, since there is no callback that a technique class can use to be informed of when a long-running execution is started. If you think that that this would be a valuable thing to have, you could add that to the ExplorationTechnique base class and integrate it with the simulation manger, or you could come up with a different solution using the tools already available.

Good luck!

This message shows up whenever the instruction pointer at the end of a block has become a symbolic value with more than 257 ways to satisfy the constraints applied to it. Usually, this means that it's unconstrained, and we have no way of continuing execution.

If you'd like to get at the successor state regardless of the fact that it can't be continued, like it seems like you want, there should be an `unconstrained_successors` attribute on `Path`, or something similar.

I am exhausted and can't really analyze that ARM right now, but I don't see any concrete difference between the construction of the successor address in the two snippets. Realistically, both of them should produce that message, since they both perform reads from memory at an unconstrained symbolic address (since you're starting from a blank state) to get the value of r3.

I agree that we need to think about this. One big thing I've just tested locally to speed up `TestQueuedSubmit` is to change how the monitoring is done. Currently, if you look at the [test timings](https://ganga-ci.cern.ch/jenkins/job/PR%20builder/935/testReport/python.Ganga.test.GPI.Parallel.TestQueuedSubmit/TestQueuedSubmit/) you see that the vast majority of the time is spent waiting for the submitted tests to finish.

Currently in tests like this, the job monitoring is done via `GangaTest.Framework.utils.sleep_until_completed` which simply prompts the monitoring system to pick up the job and then do it's thing as normal. For tests like this where the monitoring is not really the thing that is being tested, I think that a simpler approach would be useful. I've written a small replacement for `sleep_until_completed` which looks something like the following (simplified):
```python
def run_until_state(j, timeout=60, sleep_period=1):
    end_time = datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(seconds=timeout)

    while j.status != 'completed' and datetime.utcnow() < end_time:
        type(j.backend).master_updateMonitoringInformation([j])
        time.sleep(sleep_period)

    return j.status == 'completed'
```
This runs all the monitoring for the job in the main thread and runs each of them serially.

Most importantly, switching to use this implementation takes `TestQueuedSubmit` from taking 6 minutes down to taking only 1 minute.

As an alternative, perhaps monkey-patching it something like this (not sure, haven't really contributed much since the addition of CWT) could work:

```
def cwt(data, scales, *args, **kwargs):
  with tqdm(total=len(scales)) as pbar:
    tmp = pywt._cwt.integrate_wavelet
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
      pbar.update(1)
      return tmp(*args, **kwargs)
    pywt._cwt.integrate_wavelet = wrapper
    pywt.cwt(data, scales, *args, **kwargs)
```

That way, people can add whatever callback function they like to keep track of `cwt` if it is too slow.

I also got the same issue here today and found that CrawlSpider do a response type check in _requests_to_follow function

```python
def _requests_to_follow(self, response):
    if not isinstance(response, HtmlResponse):
        return
    ...
```

However responses generated by Splash would be **SplashTextResponse** or **SplashJsonResponse**. That check caused splash response won't have any requests to follow.

For intrinsics, you need to configure the device to the required color resolution (using `enable_stream`) and then call `device::get_stream_intrinsics`. The resulting object will provide helper functions to project points to pixels and vice versa, as well as FOV, principal point and focal length parameters. 
For extrinsics, you need to call `get_extrinsics` with the two streams you wish to convert between. For instance, depth and color to get the translation/rotation between depth image and color image.

UPDATE [SOLVED]: The problem it seems was the UEFI boot. During installing the pre-built libraries on Linux it askes for password to disable UEFI secure boot. Once the libraries are installed reboot the system and it will show a bluescreen. Press any key to enter the respective characters of the password you entered earlier. E.g. if password is 'avengers' it will ask you to randomly enter first. third etc character so enter 'a', 'e' etc. Then select YES for "disable secure boot?" question. Upon reboot the viewer as well as the examples started working.

Hi, If the FPS number for FishEye monotonically increases, then the frames arrive.  You can do a rough FPS approximation by dividing the frame number by timestamp (in millisec) - For FishEye the number is either 30 or 60.
In addition there are two GUI controls for FishEye that you should also try : "Exposure" and "Gain" that appear at the bottom of the sliders panel, next to "STROBE" and "External Trigger" check boxes.
And last thing, and I know that this sounds silly, but please verify that you have the FishEye lens cover plastic cap removed.

You can solve by decorating the class, like:

```python
@schema.register
class AnonymousUserType(Customer):
    pass
```

@whalesalad the example you posted for inline fragments is only applicable to `Interface`s.
In the case of `Union`s, there is no "common" fields between types (at least none that the GraphQL engine is aware of, without making some assumptions that might not be correct).
On the other hand, with an `Interface`, you assure that all it's implementations at least have the interface fields in common.

Quoted from the [official GraphQL spec](https://facebook.github.io/graphql/#sec-Unions):
> With interfaces and objects, only those fields defined on the type can be queried directly; to query other fields on an interface, typed fragments must be used. This is the same as for unions, but unions do not define any fields, so no fields may be queried on this type without the use of typed fragments.

Here's what's going on around the steep drops.

PR curves, of course, assume a threshold value that increases (non-linearly) from right to left of the chart. Hence, as the threshold increases (We move left along the X axis), we become more stringent about what we deem to be a positive example (We classify fewer examples as positive).

At high enough thresholds, we only have a handful (< 10) of FPs and TPs. Precision is TP / (TP + FP), so a difference of 1 positive classification could change precision a lot. Hence, we generally see more volatility at those regions:

![csq8ynwugjk 1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4221553/30000074-285bd2ee-9014-11e7-9783-1de8a43f7cc5.png)

Eventually, the threshold is so high that TP = 0, and so precision is 0. However, previous summary logic set a minimum of a very tiny value for the numerator,

```python
_TINY_EPISILON = 1e-7
...
precision = tf.maximum(_TINY_EPISILON, tp) / tf.maximum(
        _TINY_EPISILON, tp + fp)
```

and so the tooltip shows a tiny but non-zero value of 1e-7 at the steep drop.
![ezkmbgakvwy](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4221553/30000110-fca08270-9014-11e7-8e5a-c57425adcf9b.png)

Perhaps the shared access point should also allow for optimized SQL for quickly performing certain operations. For instance, the `is_active` methods of many plugins could simply call

```python
cursor = db.execute(
          ('SELECT 1 FROM Tags WHERE Tags.plugin_name = ? LIMIT 1'),
          (metadata.PLUGIN_NAME,))
return bool(cursor)
```

Thanks for the pointers.  From what I understand, "SmartBridge" is essentially a marketing term for using IPv6 ND Proxy to bridge IEEE 802.15.4 networks.  Such methods work with RPL non-storing networks because the RPL root maintains state of all devices in its subtree.

In contrast, Thread does not require a single node to maintain state about all devices in the network.  This is by design, to increase robustness (i.e. no single-point-of-failure) and reduce resource requirements on any one device in the network.  As a result, Thread by itself does not directly support an IPv6 ND Proxy model.  However, it is possible to layer on additional application-layer functionality (e.g. DHCPv6, unsolicited Address Notifications, application-specific messaging, etc.) that can be used to provide such information to a bridging device.  OpenThread does not currently implement such functionality, but it can be done.

@petersonjr 

* Yes, if sniffing is turned off, csvkit will use the Python defaults, which are [described here](https://docs.python.org/2/library/csv.html#dialects-and-formatting-parameters). Note that when reading, the Python defaults are flexible about whether the line terminator is `
`, `` or `
`. When writing, csvkit uses `
` instead of `
`, as you wrote.
* The [documentation for `csv.reader`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/csv.html#csv.reader) says more specific parameters override the `dialect` parameter. I did some debugging, and the issue is that the sniffer was determining that whitespace was the delimiter. If you set `--delimiter ","`, the output will be correct.

My impression was that PyPy uses an asynchronous tri-color collector that might not have captured an object that has become garbage in the current iteration (the object might have been referenced at the beginning of that GC cycle), but it will do so in the next one at the latest. Having spent quite a while with this, my practical experience was that two calls of ``gc.collect()`` are indeed consistently enough, with the failing testcases turning out to be actual leaks. So I think there is some value in having these, at least until these assumptions are actually violated.

> I'm not sure how the absolute header path would interact with packaging for various platforms.

Quick precision: the header path is not absolute, it is relative to the install prefix unless the found python version is outside of it.

> The headers are placed inside a specific Python version, while the config files are global. If multiple versions are installed at once, there would be multiple versions of the headers, while the config files would be overwritten by the last installation of pybind11, thus pointing to their Python version headers and leaving the other headers orphaned.

The issue seems to be that the tasks that call methods on the actor are getting scheduled before the actor process, so the order ends up being:
1. Wait for all tasks to finish submitting tasks.
2. Schedule the actor
3. Schedule the first task on the actor, which is usually the `get_status` call from the driver.

You can see this easily if you call `ray.get` on the `status_actor` before submitting the tasks to make sure that the actor is scheduled before passing the handle. The script should then exit almost immediately.

I'm not sure about the best way to handle this. Perhaps we should introduce a dependency on the actor's first task (the `__init__` method) for forked handles?

The reason dragging from the file listing works is that the drop callback at https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/blob/9f5f0ac2a4620e7e8b4432ed311566d8b547e0ae/packages/filebrowser/src/listing.ts#L1159 invokes `DocumentManager.open`, calling `DocumentManager._createOrOpenDocument`, which calls the opener at https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/blob/9f5f0ac2a4620e7e8b4432ed311566d8b547e0ae/packages/docmanager-extension/src/index.ts#L89, which adds it to the main area using `addToMainArea` (which then registers the widget with the application shell focus tracker).

From this chain, it appears that a drop callback must add a widget to the main area using the shell `addToMainArea`? That seems awkward - it's probably better to have the shell's main area dockpanel's drop overridden to add the new widget to the focus tracker if it's not already there?

* Don't [align-self: stretch] _Sliders, which clobbers the _box's preference.
* Don't clobber long labels: use min-width, not width.
* Distribute content in boxes by default.
* Play buttons can grow vertically, consistent with ToggleButtons (see proposal below).

### NOT FIXED

FEATURE / BUGFIX: min-width of label not Python-configurable, due to being a child element of the slider/whatever.

FEATURE / ELEGANCE: If user actually wanted a float, they'd ask for an HBox. So widgets **should use** as much of their personal width/height as possible.

.widget-hprogress { height: 100% } and .widget-vprogress { width: 100% } cause the progress bar to floodfill like a toggle button.

Sliders use custom shapes, to be investigated. Valid can use a Unicode checkmark. But a scaling Unicode Checkbox still has to be clicky (onClick, appropriate cursor).


weird mutability issue: radio buttons run away from clicks, as margin-bottom of widget-radio-box counts up like +6px mod 32px every time the widget updates. expected value is var(--jp-widgets-radio-item-height-adjustment) == 8px.

inconsistent DOM manipulation: Checkbox has an empty label / left margin regardless of description (which displays floating right). Valid widgets lose their label / left margin if description is blank.

Some cosmetic niggles that don't impact functionality:

* Selection slider value can change the slider's length, e.g. while being edited.
* Right alignment of labels on tall elements (selectmultiple) doesn't look as good as centering.
* $$floating equations$$ in HTMLMath are not centered.
* Changing the aspect ratio of an Image is not prevented (which would be opinionated).

Correct, -mavx2 allows the compiler to place avx2 code into whatever place it likes.

This piece of code looks like it compiles in avx2 unconditionally, though I am not familiar with this cython feature:
https://github.com/zarr-developers/numcodecs/blob/master/numcodecs/vlen.c#L9

If your code that profits from avx2 is inside of non-public cython code called from python it should be pretty easy to compile it twice wrap the appropriate call depending on runtime environment in python.
Code to determine cpu features at runtime can be found in e.g. blosc or you can use compiler features (like gcc and newer clang versions __builtin_cpu_supports)
This is also assuming cython cannot yet by itself do automatic cpu set specific function dispatching like gcc (or icc) can.
